text: Have you ever tried a finger lime? These are some of the most unique citrus you can grow. Due to their unique shape on the outside, they get the name finger lime, but they also have the nickname caviar lime because the inside looks just like caviar. Depending on the variety, you can get all different color, shape, sizes, and even different flavor profiles. If you all have been following along, you know that about a year ago, I planted one of these trees in the old garden, but since I recently moved, today we're digging it up and we're moving it over to the new garden. It's amazing just how fast these grow. It probably has tripled in size in just the past year. Because these trees have a whole bunch of thorns and they also happen to be pretty drought tolerant, I decided to put it all the way on the far end of the area that I'll be planting. That way, I hopefully don't run into those spines as I care for the other trees, and it doesn't need as much water and the sprinklers don't get this area that well. That being said, even though it's drought tolerant in the long run, I will be watering it frequently for the next couple weeks until it gets established. But I really think that this tree is going to thrive in this new spot. I'll be sure to make more updates on this tree as it settles into its new home, and until next time, get outside and see what you can find.

but they also have the nickname caviar lime because the inside looks just like caviar.

and even different flavor profiles.

Because these trees have a whole bunch of thorns and they also happen to be pretty drought tolerant,

That way, I hopefully don't run into those spines as I care for the other trees,
and it doesn't need as much water and the sprinklers don't get this area that well.
That being said, even though it's drought tolerant in the long run,
I will be watering it frequently for the next couple weeks until it gets established.
But I really think that this tree is going to thrive in this new spot.
I'll be sure to make more updates on this tree as it settles into its new home,
and until next time, get outside and see what you can find.
